title: Let's Create a Culture of Kindness Online
excerpt: Behind every digital gadget is a human being with real feelings. It's time we built a culture of kindness online — before our words cost someone their tomorrow.
content:
No jokes! It's time we realized that behind every digital gadget is a human being with real feelings.

Every comment, tweet, or post has the potential to uplift or crush someone's spirit. Unfortunately, online spaces often become breeding grounds for hate, bullying, and cruelty. We must change this narrative and build a culture of kindness online.

Think of the tragic case of Nigerian student Deborah Samuel, who was brutally attacked and killed after being accused of blasphemy in a WhatsApp group chat. Her story reminds us of how digital words can quickly translate into real-world violence.

Or consider Kenyan athlete Agnes Tirop, a celebrated runner who was found dead in her home, allegedly due to a toxic relationship fueled by online threats and abuse.

These are just a few of the many lives lost or severely impacted due to online hate and harassment. Many have silently suffered, their mental health deteriorating due to cyberbullying, public shaming, and cancel culture.

Recognizing Online Unkindness

Online unkindness can take different forms, many of which are normalized in digital interactions:

  • Cyberbullying — Direct insults, threats, and targeted harassment that cause emotional distress.
  • Doxxing — Sharing someone's personal information online, exposing them to danger.
  • Trolling — Posting inflammatory content meant to provoke reactions or humiliate others.
  • Cancel Culture Without Mercy — Destroying reputations without allowing room for context or redemption.
  • Passive Aggression & Gaslighting — Using sarcasm and manipulation to make others feel small or question their reality.

How Do We Cultivate Kindness in Digital Spaces?

Pause Before You Post

Ask yourself: Would I say this in person? How would I feel receiving this message? If it's not kind or constructive, don't post it. The truth is that most online bullies are timid people who in real life cannot even face their target.

Stop Body Shaming People

A lot of people are heading towards anorexia, some have bleached out their lives while some have done cosmetic surgeries — not because they needed to, but because of cyber perception and bullying.

Call Out Harmful Behaviour Respectfully

If you see someone being bullied, step in and speak up without fueling more negativity. Too often, people pile on under a hateful comment to fuel more attacks on the target. This is wrong.

See Social Media as a Tool to Spread Positivity

Instead of tearing people down, post words of encouragement, support friends' businesses, and amplify positive stories. Social media has damaged many relationships and marriages. If you have nothing good to say — keep quiet.

Practice Digital Forgiveness

Allow people room to grow from their mistakes. Everyone deserves the opportunity to learn and change. When you offend people digitally, come out digitally to apologise.

Build a Safe Online Community

Encourage respectful discussions, celebrate diversity, and report harmful content that violates community standards.

Support Those Struggling

If someone shares that they are going through a tough time, help them. Offer words of support or direct them to counsellors who can help.

Protect Your Own Peace

Unfollow or block toxic individuals who drain your energy, regardless of who they are to you. You don't have to engage in every online battle.

Let's make our online spaces places of support, learning, and love — rather than toxic platforms for pain and destruction.

The internet may be virtual, but its effects are real. Let's choose kindness — because the words we type today may determine whether someone sees tomorrow.

"Online dragging is free, but my dear, your reputation and mental health will collect the bill later."
coverImageAlt: Person looking at a phone screen with a thoughtful expression
metaTitle: Let's Create a Culture of Kindness Online | Pain2Purpose
metaDescription: Behind every screen is a real human being. Sandra Opara explores how online cruelty damages mental health and how we can choose kindness in digital spaces.

title: Love Those Who Love You
excerpt: Ada sat in front of me, twisting her fingers and forcing a smile. 'He doesn't call unless he needs help… but I still care.' The one who genuinely loved her was in the friend zone. Sound familiar?
content:
Ada sat in front of me, twisting her fingers and forcing a smile.

"He doesn't call unless he needs help… but I still care," she said.

Meanwhile, Chike — the one who brings her food at work when she's too tired to cook, the one who prays with her, sends airtime randomly, and genuinely asks how her day went — was still in her friend zone. She told me, "He's too available… I don't feel butterflies."

Butterflies? Let's be clear: in matters of love, you are allowed to choose peace.

Choose people who choose you. And no, it's not pride. It's preservation.

Why Loving Those Who Love You Heals You

Psychologically

Loving those who love you regulates your mind. It gives your nervous system a break from overthinking, second-guessing, and trying to prove you're worthy. It silences that inner critic that says "I'm not enough." You sleep better. You smile for real. You breathe again.

Emotionally

Safe love heals. You begin to laugh louder, love deeper, and walk taller. Instead of feeling anxious after every chat, you feel affirmed. You don't have to shrink to be loved. You don't have to beg to be seen.

Spiritually

God is love — and love is not manipulation. It is not withdrawal of affection. It is not stringing someone along. When you love someone who genuinely honours you, it reflects the kind of love God has for us: honest, gentle, and mutual.

Practically

Relationships cost something — your time, your money, your energy. So why invest in someone who sees you as a stopgap? Why pour into a heart that has no intention of pouring back? Love is not by force. It's not hustle. It's not charity work.

You deserve love that doesn't confuse you. You deserve love that gives back, not just takes. You deserve love that feels like rest — not constant rescue missions.

Why Do We Keep Loving People Who Don't Love Us Back?

It's a question I hear often in my counselling sessions. And the truth is, it's not because you're foolish or weak. It's because you're human.

1. Childhood Wounds and Attachment Styles

Sometimes we chase love that hurts because it feels familiar. If you grew up in a home where love felt inconsistent — present today, gone tomorrow — you may unknowingly chase people who do the same. You're not crazy. You're trying to finish a story that started years ago. But it's time to wake up.

2. The Chase Feels Like a Prize

We've been told love must be hard. Movies, songs, even elders make it seem like true love must come with pain, tears, and waiting. So when someone loves us freely, we say: "He's too soft" or "She's too available." We've confused struggle with substance.

A Challenge for You Today

Look around. Who genuinely claps when you win? Who checks on you just because? Who listens to your silence and understands your storm?

That's who deserves your heart and loyalty.

Be loyal to those who are loyal to you. Love those who love you. It's not selfish — it's smart, it's healing, and in today's world, it's sane.

Normalize choosing peace over pressure.
coverImageAlt: Two people having a warm, supportive conversation
metaTitle: Love Those Who Love You | Pain2Purpose Counselling
metaDescription: Why do we chase people who don't choose us? Sandra Opara explores the psychology of unrequited love and why choosing peace over pressure is true healing.
focusKeyword: loving people who love you back

title: Your Wipers — Finding Hope in Life's Storms
excerpt: The rain was coming down in sheets, pounding against my windshield. In that moment I realized — I wasn't just driving a car. I was driving through life itself.
content:
Today, I travelled to bring my daughter home for Easter break — and suddenly, the rains came pouring down.

The rain was coming down in sheets, pounding against my windshield like a relentless drumbeat. The skies turned a dark, foreboding grey, and the droplets pounded against the glass with a force that made the road ahead almost invisible.

The windshield wipers sprang to life, swishing back and forth with a soothing rhythm that seemed to calm my racing heart. As they cleared the rain, I felt a glimmer of hope. Maybe, just maybe, I could manage through this darkness after all.

In that moment, I realized that I wasn't just driving a car — I was driving through life itself. And just like that car, I was vulnerable to the storms that raged around me. The pain, the struggles, the doubts — they all threatened to blind me, to leave me feeling lost and alone.

We All Need Wipers

But the wipers kept swishing, clearing a path for me to see the road ahead. And I realized that I had my own wipers too — my faith, my hope, my loved ones. They are the ones who help me clear away the tears, who give me a glimpse of light when all seems lost.

When the darkness closes in and you're feeling like you're driving through a storm, remember that you have your own wipers too. They might be your inner strength, your exercise routine, your faith in God, that midnight song, or a quiet inner hope that things will get better.

Our wipers differ. Just make sure you have someone or something that uplifts you. Whatever they are — hold on to them tight, because they'll help you clear away the tears and see the road ahead.

The Storm Will Pass

When you can't see a glimmer of light, when the rain seems to be pouring down forever — just turn on your wipers. Because in the end, the storm will pass, and the sun will shine bright once more.

Today, take time to appreciate your wipers. Because whatever you take time to appreciate, appreciates in value.

What are your life wipers?

If you're going through a storm and need someone to help you see the road ahead, our individual counselling sessions are a safe space to start.
coverImageAlt: Rain on a windshield with wipers clearing the view
metaTitle: Your Wipers — Finding Hope in Life's Storms | Pain2Purpose
metaDescription: A rainy drive became a powerful lesson. Sandra Opara reflects on the faith and inner strength that help us see clearly when life's storms hit hardest.

title: We Are Spiritual Beings Having a Human Experience
excerpt: Don't let your body dictate your experiences — let your spirit reign. For the most beautiful experiences in your short time on earth, allow your spirit to soar.
content:
We are spiritual beings having a human experience. Make it worthwhile.

Don't let your body dictate your experiences — let your spirit reign.

Let Your Spirit Lead

For the most beautiful experiences in your short time on earth, allow your spirit to soar. As you do, you'll unlock depths of joy, peace, and purpose that transcend the ordinary.

So often we live from the outside in — letting circumstances, opinions, and pain define who we are and what we're capable of. But when we begin to live from the inside out, something shifts. We stop reacting and start responding. We stop surviving and start truly living.

What This Means in Practice

Living as a spiritual being doesn't mean disconnecting from the real world. It means bringing a deeper awareness into your everyday moments — how you treat others, how you speak to yourself, how you respond when life gets hard.

It means asking not just "what happened to me?" but "what is this teaching me?" It means choosing growth over bitterness, purpose over pain, and love over fear — even when it's difficult.

If you're searching for that deeper sense of meaning and direction, resilience coaching can help you reconnect with who you truly are beneath the weight of life's experiences.

In the end, I'm certain that your spirit will thank you for the incredible adventure.
coverImageAlt: Person standing peacefully in sunlight with eyes closed
metaTitle: We Are Spiritual Beings Having a Human Experience | Pain2Purpose
metaDescription: Don't let your body dictate your experience. Sandra Opara reflects on what it means to let your spirit lead and unlock deeper joy, peace, and purpose in life.

title: I Am Not Having This Conversation
excerpt: There comes a time when wisdom is not about knowing everything — but knowing what to ignore. The day I discovered the power of saying 'I am not having this conversation' changed everything.
content:
There comes a time in life when wisdom is not about knowing everything — but knowing what to ignore.

I remember the day I discovered the power of saying, "I am not having this conversation."

I was in church after service, waiting to greet the pastor, when Sister Blessing leaned in, eyes twinkling like someone with exclusive presidential gossip. "Have you heard?" she asked, lowering her voice like the Holy Spirit had just whispered breaking news to her.

I already knew where this was headed. I took a deep breath, adjusted my scarf, and said, "I am not having this conversation."

She blinked. "Why na? You holier than us now?"

No, my dear. But because some conversations will drag you somewhere you never intended to go — and before you know it, your name is flying across WhatsApp groups like election results.

Conversations You Should Walk Away From

1. The "Just Bring Small Money" Scam

"Bro, just bring 50k, and in two weeks, you go cash out 500k!" If something sounds too good to be true, it is. Money does not multiply by magic. Protect your finances and your peace.

2. Gossip That Will Cost You Your Peace

Gossip is sweet until you become the topic. Today you are laughing — tomorrow your name is trending for the wrong reasons. Rule of thumb: if you won't say it in front of them, don't say it behind them. The walls have ears, and this generation has screenshots.

3. The Emotional Blackmail Campaign

Some requests start small and grow into obligations you never agreed to. If a conversation is starting to feel like manipulation, cut it off early. You are not an ATM, and your kindness is not a weakness to be exploited.

4. Sexual Harassment Disguised as Jokes

When a so-called compliment makes you uncomfortable, it is not harmless — it is harassment. If a conversation feels wrong, exit immediately. You deserve to feel safe, and your discomfort is valid.

5. Any Conversation That Hints at Criminal Activity

If someone hints at fraud, deception, or deals that require you to compromise your integrity — vanish. Some conversations won't just ruin your reputation. They will ruin your entire life.

Your Mental Health Depends on What You Allow In

Negative words, toxic talk, and unhealthy discussions weigh down the spirit. As our elders say, "The mouth is like a drum — if you don't beat it well, it can summon trouble."

So next time someone tries to drag you into a dangerous conversation, just smile and say: "I am not having this conversation."

Setting healthy boundaries around what you engage with is not rudeness. It is self-preservation — and one of the most powerful things you can do for your mental health.

Have you ever had to walk away from a toxic conversation? Share in the comments — your experience might be exactly what someone else needs to read today.
coverImageAlt: Person calmly walking away from a difficult situation
metaTitle: I Am Not Having This Conversation | Pain2Purpose Counselling
metaDescription: Some conversations are traps. Sandra Opara uses humour and hard truth to show which discussions to walk away from — for your peace and your sanity.

title: Finding Purpose After Loss — A Journey Through Widowhood
excerpt: Your scars, spots, wrinkles, and stretch marks tell stories of life — of resilience and strength. Don't compare your behind-the-scenes to everyone else's highlight reel.
content:
Loss has a way of making the world go completely silent — and then unbearably loud, all at once.

Widowhood is one of the most profound griefs a person can carry. It is not just the loss of a person. It is the loss of a future you had already begun to imagine. It is waking up and reaching for someone who is no longer there. It is learning how to be one, when you had spent years learning how to be two.

You Are Not Broken — You Are Becoming

If you are walking through this season right now, I want you to hear this clearly: what you are feeling is not weakness. It is love in its rawest form — and love that deep deserves to be grieved fully.

But grief, while necessary, is not the final destination. It is the road.

Your scars, spots, wrinkles, and stretch marks tell stories of life — of resilience, of strength. Don't compare your behind-the-scenes moments to everyone else's highlight reels. Your journey is not behind. It is simply different.

When Pain Becomes Purpose

I have sat with many women who walked through the valley of widowhood and came out the other side not the same as they were before — but more themselves than they had ever been.

They discovered strengths they didn't know they had. They built communities for others walking the same road. They turned their grief into advocacy, their loss into legacy.

This is not toxic positivity. This is not telling you to "get over it" or "move on." This is the truth that purpose can be found even in the deepest pain — not despite it, but often because of it.

You Don't Have to Walk This Alone

One of the most important things I've learned in years of grief counselling is that healing is not a solo sport. It was never meant to be.

Community matters. Professional support matters. Allowing yourself to be held by those who understand matters.

If you are navigating loss and you're not sure where to begin, please know that our grief and loss support sessions are a safe, compassionate space where your story will be heard and honoured.

Your purpose is waiting for you to discover it. It's in the moments of vulnerability, of imperfection, of authenticity. It's in the connections you make with others, the love you share, and the stories you tell.

May we all live to fulfil purpose.
coverImageAlt: A person sitting quietly by a window in contemplation
metaTitle: Finding Purpose After Loss — A Journey Through Widowhood | Pain2Purpose
metaDescription: Loss can feel like the end. Sandra Opara shares how pain, when processed with courage and support, can become the very foundation of a purposeful life.
focusKeyword: finding purpose after grief and loss
